one_line_takeaway: By watermarking only a sparse set of POS-anchored tokens and running the detection z-test over just those positions, Sparse Watermark keeps green-list detectability (99-100% TPR) while cutting quality loss roughly in half (ROUGE-L drop ~11-14% vs 22-47% for baselines), at the cost of weaker robustness under heavy edits.
key_finding: Sparse Watermark matches baseline detectability (TPR ~99-100%, TNR ~98-100%) while substantially reducing quality degradation. On Long-form QA the ROUGE-L drop is only 11.07% (Determiner), 12.60% (Verb), 14.40% (Noun) versus 22.37% (Hard) up to 47.06% (Unigram); on Summarization the drop is 10.74%-21.64% for Sparse versus 29.14%-50.87% for baselines. All three Sparse variants take the top-3 quality spots. Semantic similarity is highest for Sparse (0.836 Verb, 0.814 Determiner, 0.794 Noun) versus 0.652-0.765 for baselines. Determiner is best for quality because its watermarked positions have high next-token entropy (Fig 4) and it watermarks fewer tokens. On robustness, Sparse is competitive but not dominant: at 10% substitution it is best/near-best (Sparse-Noun 100.0), but at 50% substitution it degrades more (Sparse-Determiner 67.6, Sparse-Verb 72.4) than SelfHash (92.3) and LeftHash (83.9). Against DIPPER paraphrasing, Sparse-Determiner is near state-of-the-art (74.3% at 40L, 66.9% at 40L-40O), only 0.7 and 2.6 percentage points behind SelfHash.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Sparse Watermark, a new category of LLM watermarking that embeds the watermark into only a small, structurally-anchored SUBSET of generated tokens rather than every token. The key idea is to anchor each watermarked token to the Part-of-Speech (POS) tag of the preceding word: during generation, whenever the last completed word has a POS tag in a chosen set I (e.g. VERB, NOUN, or DET), the vocabulary is split into Green/Red lists via the standard hash and the NEXT token is forced to come only from the Green list; otherwise the token is sampled from the model's unmodified distribution. Because the watermarked positions are recoverable from POS structure at detection time, the z-test is computed ONLY over the sparse watermarked positions (T becomes the count of watermarked positions, not total tokens), which preserves detection strength while leaving most of the text unperturbed. This is the authors' own contribution and is distinct from the Kirchenbauer et al. green-list scheme it builds on: rather than tuning a logit bias delta, Sparse Watermark uses hard Green-list restriction (no delta) on a sparse set of POS-anchored positions. It is training-free.
threat_model: Explicitly stated (Section 3.2) and taken from prior work (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023, Zhao et al. 2024, Liu et al. 2024a). Goal: embed a watermark so a party can later verify whether given text was generated by the watermarked LLM (detecting misuse such as fake news or assignment cheating). Adversary: aware that a watermark is present and attempts to evade detection; may have access to both open-source and private (non-watermarked) models to produce alternate text; is restricted to modifications that erase the watermark WITHOUT significantly changing the original semantics of the sentence. Concretely evaluated attacks: synonym substitution (RoBERTa-Large masked-token replacement at 10/30/50% of tokens) and paraphrasing via DIPPER (an 11B T5-XXL-based paraphraser) at lexical-diversity 40 (40L) and lexical+order diversity 40 (40L-40O).
limitations: Robustness is the weak point: under strong perturbation (50% synonym substitution) most Sparse variants fall well behind SelfHash/LeftHash because deleting/altering the POS-anchor words removes watermarked positions; only the Determiner variant is competitive against paraphrasing, while Verb/Noun variants are poor under DIPPER (43.5-54.3% at 40L-40O, near Hard's 41.0). QMSum (shortest answers) is a consistent failure mode for robustness across all methods. Evaluation is single-model (Llama2-7B-chat only) and English-only, and relies on an external POS parser at both generation and detection time (correctness/consistency of decoding hinges on the parser reproducing the same tags; the paper notes it must map Universal tags to Penn Treebank sub-tags). The method assumes the attacker preserves enough sentence structure for POS anchors to survive; no adaptive attacker that specifically targets/rewrites the anchoring POS words is evaluated. The security-strength/robustness trade-off is shifted, not eliminated. Code was listed as 'to be published soon' at submission.

datasets_used: WaterBench evaluation protocol (Tu et al. 2023), long-answer subset only
quantitative_results: Sparse-Determiner: QA R-L 19.20 (down 11.07%), Summ R-L 20.89 (down 10.99%), TPR 100.0/100.0, semantic sim 0.814
Sparse-Verb: QA R-L 18.87 (down 12.60%), Summ R-L 20.95 (down 10.74%), TPR 100.0/100.0, semantic sim 0.836 (highest)
Sparse-Noun: QA R-L 18.48 (down 14.40%), Summ R-L 18.39 (down 21.64%), TPR 100.0/100.0, semantic sim 0.794
Baseline quality drops (QA/Summ): Hard 22.37%/29.14% (sim 0.765), LeftHash 32.61%/43.20% (sim 0.693), SelfHash 40.94%/46.57% (sim 0.663), Unigram 47.06%/50.87% (sim 0.652)
Substitution attack TPR (Sparse-Verb): 99.8 (10%), 96.3 (30%), 72.4 (50%); SelfHash for comparison: 99.8/98.1/92.3
DIPPER paraphrasing TPR: Sparse-Determiner 74.3 (40L) / 66.9 (40L-40O); SelfHash 75.0 / 69.5 (best); Sparse-Verb 54.3 / 43.5
Sparse gamma=0.05 gives 100% TPR for Verb/Noun/Determiner (Table 5); baselines use gamma 0.25-0.5 with delta 10-15
POS document frequency (Table 6): VERB/DET/NOUN all 100.0%, PUNCT 99.8%, ADP 99.6%, ADJ 99.0%, NUM 67.7%, X 13.0%
Qualitative QMSum example (Table 3): SelfHash z=16.99 but semantic sim 0.298 (garbled text); Sparse-Determiner z=11.53 with semantic sim 0.726
baselines_compared: Hard watermark (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023, green-list restriction)
LeftHash / Soft watermark (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023, logit-bias delta)
Unigram watermark (Zhao et al. 2024, fixed green list)
